messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Detail the dissemination plan, exploitation roadmap, IP strategy, and open access commitments.
Write the Dissemination, exploitation and communication section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.2 M € - - In short, we have laid the foundation for achieving the expected impact of our project: an innovative research agenda, a resourceful action plan for the contextualisation and exploitation of the expected results, and an excellent conglomerate of actors in our consortium who are in pursuit of a shared common goal. 

2.2 Measures to maximise impacts Our vision is to lead the transition towards a sustainable post-petroleum society in the plastic sector while decoupling economic growth from resource depletion and environmental impact by serving industry with the provision of cost-effective high performance sustainable multifunctional bioplastic packaging solutions with tailored materials and organic recyclability and biogas potential at EoL. In order to assist Europe in the transition towards a bio-economy, we will establish new sustainable cross-sectorial value chains providing competitive bio-based alternatives in lightweight packaging, such as nets and trays and textile applications. In line with a number of EU and national regulations, we will address the use of non-biodegradable shopping bags and lightweight multipurpose bags as well as those banning potentially polluting single-use items, e.g. disposable tableware (e.g. in France), but also other enforcement measures for the broader EU SET plan 137, combined with increasing incentives for significant bio-sourcing (e.g. in Germany). There is a clear need for new materials with high bio-based content and for meeting the demand for plastics with innovative EoL options. In order to realise this vision, we have carefully planned post-project exploitation pathways already during our BIOnTOP proposal preparation. We have devised our initial strategy for the dissemination and the exploitation of our project results, with a view to increasing our projects’ innovation & business outcome as well as social & environmental impacts. 

Outline of the dissemination plan Our dissemination plan for BIOnTOP comprises a set of actions to spread the non-confidential results generated during our project to ensure that as many relevant audiences as possible have access to them. The aim underpinning our dissemination strategy is to heighten awareness about the tangible and intangible outputs of BIOnTOP, to contribute to knowledge building and advancement of innovation in Europe as well as to avoid duplication of research work. In order to safeguard the commercial opportunity, our dissemination activities will be subject to a confidentiality policy. By default, and building on our consortium experience in collaborative projects, any result of our project is to be treated as confidential and can be disseminated only after agreement of the project partners following the relevant clauses of our consortium agreement (signed during the grant agreement preparation phase in keeping with initial agreements on ownership reflected herein and respecting the general terms of the grant agreement). This approach allows us to retain complete control and make sure that no “exploitable” information is disclosed before being protected. The protection of the IPR is of invaluable importance for us, the participating partners. Therefore, the non-confidential results and information on our project to be publicly disseminated will be carefully identified. The RTD participants in our consortium will publish results in peer-reviewed open access journals, which will be an indicator of overall project success (in terms of progress vs. the scientific state of the art) and an important element of dissemination towards industry, standardisation bodies, and policy makers. Our industrial partners and market actors will focus on showcasing and disseminating results at conferences and trade fairs and through other industrial channels such as technical/sectorial magazines, industry network meetings, and the like (to demonstrate the industrial novelty and commercial potential of our project outcomes). The IPR Manager (Dr. Marco de la Feld from ENCO) will work closely with us, the project innovators, to manage the identification of non-confidential disclosable information that can be published/disseminated. He will lead us, the project partners, in strategically planning and effectively implementing the dissemination during a dedicated task in our Work Plan, Task 7.5 Exploitation and Dissemination Planning. All the measures (outlined in table 10) will be tailored taking into account our project needs, the target groups’ background and the expertise of our project partners. Our project dissemination activities will intersect dissemination strategies of the BBI-JU, via full members BioMi and PLANET (in process) and associated BIC members AIMPLAS, INSTM, CTB and EUBP, as well as other EU PPP as SPIRE through IRIS and ENCO, which will contribute to the distribution of our project outputs across the relevant European and international communities and amplify our project dissemination effort. All our dissemination activities will be developed with an eye towards the exploitation of our project results by other parties, in order to lay down the basis for subsequent innovation actions as well as other private initiatives which can accelerate the route towards the take-up of new technologies within the bio-based plastic sector, also beyond our BIOnTOP selected applications. Beyond the above discussed use of traditional dissemination channels (presentations & posters at conferences, publications, industry magazines, newsletters and online platforms), we will also tap into more vanguard channels, such as social media platforms (ResearchGate, Academia.edu, SlideShare, Twitter, YouTube, Vision2020 open innovation platform), etc. Also, our project website will serve as a lasting virtual hub for disseminating downloadable public deliverables and outputs of BIOnTOP, as it will house also the ‘BIOnTOP Green Channel’ for open access to the peer-reviewed publications of our project. In particular, the network of EUBP across the European bioplastics value chain should be highly instrumental in identifying and approaching prospective business partners and potential licensees. With around 70 member companies from across the bioplastics value chain that are active in the European market, EUBP works closely together with all major stakeholders in this industry. The association also has access to several unparalleled channels of communication within the bioplastics industry, ranging from the largest European bioplastics conference, to the bioplastics-focused newsletter with the widest circulation, to one of the most widely-followed (4000+ followers) Twitter accounts focusing on bioplastics topics. To amplify our impacts, we also capitalise on ISWA’s (in our AB) communication channels to reach the waste management sector.

2.2.2 IPR management  
Our IPR management of the FreeMe project will be based on three pillars: Documentation of agreements, direct actions for the management of the IP, and necessary interactive activities. The two legal documents defining the main aspects of our IPR management are the Grant Agreement (GA) signed between the EC and us and the Consortium Agreement (CA) signed among us. In the latter, all aspects related to the background knowledge of us that are necessary for the implementation of our project as well as for the post-project exploitation of the results will be defined in detail. In general, we have already agreed that access to the background knowledge, IPRs, and research data will be royalty-free during our project implementation, while the post-project exploitation will be done under fair conditions.  
The general IP management actions will be undertaken by Incotec. The ownership of the foreground knowledge will be depicted in the final “Result Ownership List” (ROL) and will be decided between us consensually based on which partners have been involved in the generation of these results. The communication between the IPR manager and us through appropriate meetings and questionnaires will define efficiently the foreground knowledge. In general, the owner of the IP belongs to the partner that develops it. Joint ownership will be accepted in the case that the results have been jointly generated; however, in this case, the joint owners will agree on the allocation and terms of exploitation of this joint ownership in communication with Incotec and the Coordinator. We will be fully responsible for the protection of our foreground ownership in respect to our own legitimate interests, as well as the legitimate interests of the other partners. We have already agreed that results with a promising commercial or industrial exploitation capacity will be protected. All the IPR updates as well as the Key Results and their IP routes presented in the business case will be included in the PEDR deliverables in terms of Task 8.3.  
Regarding the interactive activities with our consortium, we will organize an internal IPR seminar for the presentation of the different IP protection types and exploitation routes for each Key Result in the first year (M6) as well as a final IPR seminar on M46 for the presentation of the ROL. Market potential of our GREEN-LOOP products  
Bottle closures: The main industry segments for bottle closures are food and beverages packaging and pharmaceuticals. The main material used for bottle closures and caps is plastic, over 54% in 2020, followed by metal, increasing market position due to being more sustainable and durable than plastic, and other materials, such as wood or glass. Emerging segments that will propel the demand for plastic bottle closures and caps are estimated to be i) healthcare, ii) cosmetics and toiletries, iii) automotive industry, iv) cleaning products.  
•Multifunctional rubber panels: The global natural rubber market demand reached a volume of 15.2 million metric tons in 2020. The demand is expected to grow at a CAGR of 4.8% in the forecast period of 2021-2026. The demand for natural rubber is expected to reach a volume of 20.1 million metric tons by 2026. The Rubber sheets industry is characterized by having intense competition and producers. Key money-spinning factors are driving the restraints for the growing market which can be overpassed with strategic planning, as stated in the report.  
•Wood composite bearings: The composite bearings market is expected to grow at a CAGR of over 6% during 2020-2025. The growth in demand for composite bearings from the automotive industry is driving the market. Nevertheless, high raw material costs for manufacturing composite bearings and unfavorable conditions arising due to the COVID-19 outbreak are hindering the growth of the market. The sector that will govern the global market for composite bearings is automotive, with Asia-Pacific being the largest region and the fastest-growing market.  

Business model  
The different business models for our GREEN-LOOP results will target construction, automotive, and food and beverage, the main market segments globally for the upscale of our developed products and technologies, as shown in the different reports. Other promising segments will be further explored as agriculture, healthcare, and cosmetics within our project’s implementation, finding potential applications beyond our project’s life. Specific value propositions will be defined based on delivering the value of the innovative sustainable products at an appropriate and competitive cost. Figure 18 shows the canvas of our sustainable business model using a 3-bottom line (social, economy, and life cycle) approach for measuring performance. This scheme considers the needs of stakeholders, gives priority to shareholders, and promotes environmental stewardship.  

Marketing strategy  
•Biobased polymers: The growth rate for bio-based polymers with 8% CAGR far above overall polymer market growth, expected to maintain until 2025. International companies are already increasing their service catalogue to include sources of renewable carbon, mainly biomass, CO2, and recycling, boosting the bio-based and biodegradable polymers demand. However, there is still a lack of support from governments and policies. Only a few countries such as Italy, France, and probably Spain will politically support this additional disposal path. The market opportunities are still rising for bioplastics as the demand is continuously growing, with new applications and products, and biodegradable plastics production is likely to increase to 1.8 million in 2025, more than 80% compared to 2019.  
•Thermoplastic elastomer market: The global Thermoplastic Elastomer Market size was USD 26,856.8 million in 2019 and is projected to reach USD 39,424.6 million by 2027, exhibiting a CAGR of 5.7% during 2021-2027. In 2020, the TPE market declined by 18.2% in terms of volume, compared to 2019, due to the COVID-19 pandemic that impacted the supply chain and demand reduction of end-use industries, which impacted TPE manufacture. The market trend focuses on innovation in low-cost and high-performance materials from industries, led by the automotive industry, with a market consumption of over 43% of the global demand for TEPs in 2019.  
•Biocomposites: The global composites market size is projected to grow from USD 74.0 billion in 2020 to USD 112.8 billion by 2025, at a CAGR of 8.8%. Being the global biocomposites market size projected to reach USD 46.3 billion by 2025, exhibiting a 12.5% CAGR. The main market segments are automotive, building and construction, and electrical and electronics, and China is leading the manufacturing market. Market drivers are high technical properties and high demand in end users, thus innovation offers opportunities for market investments. The transport industry, automotive, and aerospace have the largest volume composites shares in the global market. In Europe and North America, the demand for products that are 100% biodegradable is growing, increasing the use of natural polymer, opening a full range for market opportunities.

2.2 Measures to maximise impact Measures to maximise impact will be delivered via WP7 Communication, Dissemination and Exploitation, led by accelCH and closely supported by NEBIH, NP and ROMER. Support and feedback on the planned measures and their long-term impact for commercial and non-commercial exploitation will be received from a dedicated Innovation Manager. The primary goal of our multi-disciplinary and cross-sectorial consortium in WP7 is to disseminate the research results generated in our scientific work packages (WP1-WP6), to raise awareness and provide information on our novel photon-based technology, through communication and outreach activities, and to pave the way for exploitation of the PHOTONFOOD innovations and IP for medium and long-term non-commercial and commercial purposes such as detection and analysis of (micro)biological and chemical contamination along the food production chain from ‘farm to fork’. An overview of our dissemination, exploitation, and communication activities is detailed in the following sections (see 2.2.1 and 2.2.2). 

2.2.1 Dissemination and exploitation of results To maximise impact, we have defined a project-specific dissemination, exploitation and communication strategy that builds on the following three main points. (1) Stakeholder involvement and target-group orientation: To ensure that the interests and requirements of external audiences are fully taken into consideration during the delivery of the PHOTONFOOD project. Along the whole food value chain ’from farm to fork’, we will actively involve representatives of our key target audiences (farmers and novel food producers, food processors, food retailers and wholesalers, analytical labs, EU entry points, technology providers, photonic companies), from the beginning of the project to ensure technology acceptance (WP1), validation in a realistic setting (WP6) and outreach (WP7). The proposed target-group orientation allows us to define and implement measures that are geared to the specific needs of the different audiences (Fig. 12). Additionally, PHOTONFOOD will be in close contact with relevant food safety authorities and will work towards strengthening the community, uniting many important players in the food supply chain. We will aim to collect feedback from interested and influential stakeholders, as well as related projects, and disseminate our results and achievements to them, in order to maximise impact. (2) Cross-channel and multi-media approach: To guarantee the widest possible outreach, we will use different channels to inform about the PHOTONFOOD project and to disseminate research results and innovation and technology developments. These range from direct or face-to-face contact with scientists at conferences and webinars and industrial workshops organized in WP1 to indirect information distributed to all stakeholders. Additionally, we will take advantage of print media, digital tools and platforms for our dissemination, training, and communication activities. Some of our digital outreach highlights include video clips, open webinars, education and marketing videos. (3) Open access and open data policy: To enable the future uptake of PHOTONFOOD’s research results and to facilitate future sustainability of the materials and technologies, we are fully committed to open access publishing, following the Guidelines on Open Access to Scientific Publications and Research Data in Horizon 2020 that are in line with current trends in IP management and publication strategies (see section 2.2.1.3). This will be achieved by (i) applying existing standards for the development and results publication, (ii) involving stakeholders as of the beginning of the action and (iii) publishing project results in peer-reviewed journals while ensuring open access. 

Plan for dissemination and exploitation of results The WP7 leader accelCH will coordinate and monitor the related tasks and closely cooperate with all project partners and external collaborators involved. We will be responsible for monitoring and coordination of the implementation of the defined outreach strategy, through the dissemination activities, exploitation plans, and communication actions at the consortium level with all work package leaders offering support related to dissemination, communication and exploitation of our project and its outcomes. 2.2 MEASURES TO MAXIMIZE IMPACT  
We have already planned a credible path to maximize the impact of our project actions. This plan is comprised of five primary and interdependent elements, namely: (i) specification of target groups, (ii) dissemination activities, (iii) communication channels and activities, (iv) exploitation strategy for market penetration, (v) intellectual property and regulatory assessment.  
Before describing in detail the individual elements, it is noted that both dissemination and communication activities will be led by ISWA, the largest waste management network worldwide, with the mission to promote and develop sustainable and professional waste management and catalyse the transition to a circular economy. ISWA has already established an extensive network of different stakeholders in the waste management sector that will be actively informed about RECLAIM, our activities and results. Along this line, we will regularly present our project in the annual ISWA World Congress, which is the industry’s leading solid waste management event. Exploiting direct access to the focused, global waste management and circular economy community, we will assure maximum impact for the proposed work and high potential to promptly include prMRF in the material recovery technologies used globally.  
It is noted that, during the last phase of our project (Wrap up & Consolidation phase), we will consider the transfer and live demonstration of the prMRF in the ISWA World Congress and/or other high-visibility exhibitions in the circular economy domain. This unique demonstration opportunity will be possible due to the portability and quick installation of the prMRF that permits the implementation of demonstration tours to increase our project visibility and spark the interest of stakeholders and investors in prMRF and the relevant project technologies.  
Exploitation activities will be led by AXIA, which has long experience in designing strategies and implementing activities that overcome the barriers for the market uptake of research results. Before the end of our project, AXIA will organize a major event to present RECLAIM results to stakeholders and demonstrate prMRF to investors and potential customers.

2.2 Measures to maximise impact - Dissemination, exploitation and communication First version of the Plan for the dissemination, exploitation and communication (PDEC): Our planned measures to maximise the impact of the NIPULOOP project are outlined in this initial PDEC. It will be further detailed by M6 (D9.1) and periodically updated in line with project progress and requirements across its 3 project phases, as follows: Setting the foundations: Creating a strong visual identity for NIPULOOP as a circular, digitally enabled PU innovation initiative, alongside a DEC strategy focused on building a cross -sector community around circular polymers, advanced materials, DPP & FAIR data ecosystems. This includes long-term stakeholder engagement to avoid fragmented efforts and interactive activities targeting industry, recyclers, regulators and end-users (with a primary focus on mobility value chains where early adoption potential is highest) to foster understanding, trust and mobilisation around circular NIPU solutions. Phase 1: Kick -off phase of DEC activity implementation to raise awareness about the project, including positioning NIPULOOP within the European advanced materials and circular economy landscape (IAM4EU, Made in Europe, Materials Commons for Europe) and engaging early with key stakeholders across the NIPU value chain. Phase 2: DEC activities to disseminate intermediate results and demonstrate progress in NIPU materials development, industrial processing and circularity pathways, while building links with other projects, stakeholders and actors across the ecosystem. There will be focus on clustering activities, standardisation engagement and alignment with emerging frameworks such as DPPs and SSbD methodologies. Phase 3: DEC activities to intensify dissemination of validated results , incl. TRL6 demonstrator outcomes in the mobility sector & closed-loop recycling validation, alongside planning and implementing pathways to exploit and valorise project outcomes and Key Exploitable Results (KERs) . This will include industrial uptake strategies, cross -sector replication potential and engagement with investors, industry adopters, policymakers to support post-project deployment. Communication plan: We have defined a strategic and continuous communications approach built around the following clear communication goals and objectives: ■Raise societal awareness of the role of safe, circular and sustainable polymer materials, highlighting the transition from conventional PU to non-isocyanate, recyclable alternatives and their importance for health, worker health and safety, environment and resource efficiency. ■Engage industrial stakeholders and end -users (directly and via associations, clusters and networks) to communicate NIPULOOP’s relevance for enabling circular, digitally traceable polyurethane value chains, supporting industrial competitiveness, regulatory compliance (e.g. MDR, SSbD), and scalable ad option across sectors such as mobility, medical devices, construction. ■Establish synergies with external bodies, including standardisation and policy-related organisations, contributing to the development of frameworks for circular polymers, Digital Product Passports (DPP), FAIR data practices and SSbD implementation, ensuring alignment with European initiatives such as IAM4EU, Made in Europe and Materials Commons for Europe. ■Achieve high visibility within the scientific and technical community , reinforcing credibility, stimulating collaboration, and ensuring that NIPULOOP contributes to advancing the state of the art in circular polymer chemistry, NIPU systems, digitalised materials development and closed -loop recycling technologies, including through joint activities with other EU-funded projects. Communication toolbox and channels: To achieve these objectives, a dedicated Communication Toolbox will be developed and maintained, consisting of the following: ■ Visual identity and branding: A project logo has been developed in proposal preparation. This will form the basis of a brand book with visual guidelines to ensure consistency across all outputs; for building a professional image. ■ Project website and social media: The project website will serve as the central communication hub, hosting public deliverables, news, blog content (e.g. >4 Thought Leadership articles and >2 white papers), ≥ 6 newsletters and multimedia content. User needs and market drivers : The demand for NIPU is already being driven by a combination of regulatory, industrial, and supply chain pressures. At user level (OEMs, Tier 1 suppliers, formulators), the key needs include: compliance with tightening regulations on isocyanates and VOC emissions ; safer materials for workers and manufacturing environments; compatibility with existing production lines ; stable performance at industrial scale ; traceability of materials and recycled content; access to circular material solutions that can be verified and reported In parallel, procurement behaviour is changing . Sustainability targets are now directly influencing purchasing decisions, with over 60% of applications already achieving performance parity with conventional PU, and major OEMs setting targets for sustainable material sourcing by 2030. However, adoption is today uneven because: processability is not always predictable; scale-up routes are not fully stabilised; recycling is not yet proven at system level; specifications and qualification routes are still emerging Fit to market and cost -effectiveness: NIPULOOP addresses these gaps directly by focusing not only on material development, but on making NIPU usable under real industrial conditions NIPULOOP will align with market needs in three ways: 1) Industrial compatibility: Formulations are developed to run on existing PU processing routes (REX, RIM, RTM, coatings), avoiding the need for new capital investment. This will be a key condition for adoption in the mobility sector ; 2) Process reliability and scalability : Through structure–property–process modelling, QbD, and real -time monitoring (PAT), the project reduces variability and improves reproducibility-two of the main blockers for industrial uptake; 3) Circularity with measurable value : Depolymerisation and reintegration of recovered building blocks are validated, allowing materials to re -enter production so that circularity can be quantified and integrated into procurement and compliance frameworks. From a cost perspective, while NIPU still carries a 25–40% raw material premium, the value proposition is supported by: avoided regulatory costs and liabilities ; reduced VOC compliance costs ; potential for recycled feedstock substitution; alignment with OEM sustainability requirements Willingness to Buy (WTB): WTB for NIPU-based materials is already emerging and is being driven less by early- stage innovation interest and more by concrete procurement pressures across industrial value chains. The shift towards circular economy models, net-zero manufacturing and safer chemical use is directly influencing purchasing decisions, particularly in sectors such as mobility and electronics where large OEMs have committed to sourcing 100% sustainable polymers by 2030. This will create a clear pull- effect across Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers, who are required to align with these specifications. NIPU responds directly to these requirements by eliminating isocyanates and enabling the use of bio -based and CO₂ -derived feedstocks, while also supporting Scope 3 emission reduction strategies that are increasingly embedded in supplier selection criteria. Market data reflects this transition: bio-based NIPU feedstocks already represented approximately $320 million in 2025 (17.8% of the total NIPU market) and are projected to reach ~28.5% by 2034 as supply scales and costs decrease. More broadly, over 68% of Fortune 500 chemical buyers now integrate sustainability criteria into procurement decisions, indicating that WTB is no longer conditional on future regulation but is already shaping current purchasing behaviour. In this context, NIPULOOP is positioned to respond to an existing and growing demand, provided that performance, process compatibility and cost thresholds are met at industrial scale. Competitive position and advantage : The competitive landscape includes large chemical players ( Dow, BASF, Covestro, Huntsman, Evonik), as well as alternative material systems (bio -based epoxies, thermoplastics, acrylics). User needs and market drivers : The demand for NIPU is already being driven by a combination of regulatory, industrial, and supply chain pressures. At user level (OEMs, Tier 1 suppliers, formulators), the key needs include: compliance with tightening regulations on isocyanates and VOC emissions ; safer materials for workers and manufacturing environments; compatibility with existing production lines ; stable performance at industrial scale ; traceability of materials and recycled content; access to circular material solutions that can be verified and reported In parallel, procurement behaviour is changing . Sustainability targets are now directly influencing purchasing decisions, with over 60% of applications already achieving performance parity with conventional PU, and major OEMs setting targets for sustainable material sourcing by 2030. However, adoption is today uneven because: processability is not always predictable; scale-up routes are not fully stabilised; recycling is not yet proven at system level; specifications and qualification routes are still emerging Fit to market and cost -effectiveness: NIPULOOP addresses these gaps directly by focusing not only on material development, but on making NIPU usable under real industrial conditions NIPULOOP will align with market needs in three ways: 1) Industrial compatibility: Formulations are developed to run on existing PU processing routes (REX, RIM, RTM, coatings), avoiding the need for new capital investment. This will be a key condition for adoption in the mobility sector ; 2) Process reliability and scalability : Through structure–property–process modelling, QbD, and real -time monitoring (PAT), the project reduces variability and improves reproducibility-two of the main blockers for industrial uptake; 3) Circularity with measurable value : Depolymerisation and reintegration of recovered building blocks are validated, allowing materials to re -enter production so that circularity can be quantified and integrated into procurement and compliance frameworks. From a cost perspective, while NIPU still carries a 25–40% raw material premium, the value proposition is supported by: avoided regulatory costs and liabilities ; reduced VOC compliance costs ; potential for recycled feedstock substitution; alignment with OEM sustainability requirements Willingness to Buy (WTB): WTB for NIPU-based materials is already emerging and is being driven less by early- stage innovation interest and more by concrete procurement pressures across industrial value chains. The shift towards circular economy models, net-zero manufacturing and safer chemical use is directly influencing purchasing decisions, particularly in sectors such as mobility and electronics where large OEMs have committed to sourcing 100% sustainable polymers by 2030. This will create a clear pull- effect across Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers, who are required to align with these specifications. NIPU responds directly to these requirements by eliminating isocyanates and enabling the use of bio -based and CO₂ -derived feedstocks, while also supporting Scope 3 emission reduction strategies that are increasingly embedded in supplier selection criteria. Market data reflects this transition: bio-based NIPU feedstocks already represented approximately $320 million in 2025 (17.8% of the total NIPU market) and are projected to reach ~28.5% by 2034 as supply scales and costs decrease. More broadly, over 68% of Fortune 500 chemical buyers now integrate sustainability criteria into procurement decisions, indicating that WTB is no longer conditional on future regulation but is already shaping current purchasing behaviour. In this context, NIPULOOP is positioned to respond to an existing and growing demand, provided that performance, process compatibility and cost thresholds are met at industrial scale. Competitive position and advantage : The competitive landscape includes large chemical players ( Dow, BASF, Covestro, Huntsman, Evonik), as well as alternative material systems (bio -based epoxies, thermoplastics, acrylics). NIPULOOP positions itself within this landscape not by competing on chemistry alone, but by delivering a complete, industrially validated materials platform. NIPULOOP will offer a circular materials platform combining non-isocyanate chemistry based on cyclic carbonate– amine systems, with industrial processing validation (not limited to laboratory formulations), predictable process windows and reproducibility, i ntegration with existing manufacturing infrastructure, and validated recycling and reintegration routes. Business case and exploitation strategy : A comprehensive business case will quantify the value proposition of intelligent, circular vitrimer composite systems for the European construction sector and beyond. A draft business case framework will be developed in Phase 1 and delivered as part of the exploitation strategy deliverable at M18, with progressive validation in Phase 2 and final consolidation in Phase 3. A preliminary outline is provided here. Market size and potential : The European construction sector is undergoing a structural transition driven by the convergence of decarbonisation, digitalisation, and circular economy policies. Advanced composite materials, particularly FRPCs, are increasingly adopted due to their corrosion resistance, lightweight properties, and durability. The global FRPC market was valued at approximately €89 billion in 2023 and is projected to reach €129 billion by 2030, driven by demand for lightweight, corrosion -resistant structural materials across construction, transport, and energy sectors. Constructi on currently accounts for approximately 25% of FRPC consumption, representing an addressable market of roughly €22 billion that is expected to grow inline with the overall market trajectory. This construction FRPC segment sits within a broader €1.38 trilli on European construction market, in which advanced composites remain under-penetrated relative to steel and concrete. The convergence of regulatory pressure (revised CPR, ESPR, DBL and GPP criteria , etc. ) and performance advantages (corrosion resistance, design flexibility, reduced maintenance) positions intelligent circular composites as a high-growth segment within this market. MOSAIC targets three primary addressable market segments (each characterised by high exposure to environmental degradation, increasing adoption of composite alternatives, and strong demand for reduced maintenance) derived from these figures: Infrastructure rehabilitation and new build (bridges, facades, structural panels): European bridge infrastructure alone represents a maintenance and replacement market exceeding €10 billion annually (derived from the €130-150 billion annual infrastructure maintenance expenditure cited above, with bridge structures accounting for approximately 7-10%). MOSAIC's self-healing, reversible, and monitorable composite systems directly address this segment by offering ≥30% operational maintenance cost reduction and ≥20% service life extension. Prefabricated modular construction elements: The European prefabricated building market, a sub-segment of the €1.38 trillion construction sector, represents a growing opportunity for lightweight, circular composite modules that can be assembled, disassembled, and reconfigured. MOSAIC's reversible adhesive joints and modular design philosophy are directly aligned with this segment. Cross-sector replication (wind energy, automotive, marine): The vitrimer platform's circularity and self-healing properties address the €4-5billion European wind turbine blade market, where end-of-life composite waste is an acute regulatory and environmental challenge, as well as automotive and marine structural applications. This is a broad and scalable market opportunity. User needs & market gaps: Despite the growth of composites, critical gaps remain: 1) infrastructure owners face high maintenance costs and operational uncertainty due to limited real-time visibility of structural conditions. Maintenance remains reactive and inspection driven. 2) the lack of circularity in thermoset composites creates a major environmental and regulatory challenge, particularly as end-of-life waste increases. 3) existing solutions are fragmented. Materials, monitoring technologies and digital tools are developed independently, limiting integration and adoption. 4) regulatory & societal pressures are increasing, requiring transparency, safety, and sustainability across the full lifecycle of construction materials. MOSAIC addresses these gaps through the integration of self -healing materials, embedded sensing, AI-driven DT capabilities, and circularity frameworks into a unified system. VALUE PROPOSITION: MOSAIC delivers measurable value to asset owners: ≥30% lower maintenance costs, ≥20% longer service life, and recoverable material value at end -of-life—enabled by intelligent, self -healing and circular composite systems. Willingness to buy (WTB): The WTB for the MOSAIC solutions will be driven by a clear and quantifiable value proposition (VP) for infrastructure owners, contractors & asset managers, centred on lifecycle cost reduction, operational efficiency and regulatory compliance. In contrast to conventional materials, where procurement decisions are primarily based on upfront CAPEX, MOSAIC will enable a shift towards lifecycle -driven decision -making, where total cost of ownership, maintenance burden, and residual value become the dominant factors. This is particularly relevant in the context of increasing regulatory pressure and ageing infrastructure across Europe, where asset performance and durability directly impact long-term investment strategies. As such, the business case will be structured around the following economic value drivers: ▪ CAPEX/OPEX impact: Quantification of capital expenditure for vitrimer composite manufacturing lines (pultrusion, VARI, RTM) relative to conventional steel/concrete fabrication, and operational expenditure savings MOSAIC HORIZON-CL4-2025-05-MATERIALS-42-two-stage Page 29 of 53 derived from prescriptive maintenance (≥30% OPEX reduction target for operational maintenance costs enabled by prescriptive DT and self -healing) and from lifecycle-wide efficiency gains (>10% OPEX reduction target per LCSA scope, assessed following ISO 156 86-5). Initial estimates from the project's techno -economic models will be validated against demonstrator-level data. ▪ Lifecycle gains: Whole-life cost analysis based on LCCA (ISO 15686-5) incorporating extended service life (≥20% life extension), reduced maintenance interventions, and residual value recovery through reversible disassembly and chemical recycling (>96% fibre recovery, ≥80% property retention). The circularity premium -arising from reuse of components across ≥3 reprocessing cycles -will be quantified against conventional thermoset FRPCs and steel/concrete benchmarks. ▪ Payback period: Target payback of <5 years for building owners and infrastructure operators, accounting for material cost (<€10/kg for vitrimer resins), reduced labour requirements (>50% on -site labour reduction), accelerated construction (≥30% faster), and deferred replacement costs. The business case targets a payback period of <5 years for infrastructure operators, supported by the combined value of maintenance cost reduction (≥30% OPEX), extended service life (≥20%), and circularity premiums from component reuse and high-value fibre recovery. Competition landscape and competitive positioning: The current market is characterised by partial and fragmented solutions. Advanced composites offer performance but lack circularity. SHM systems provide data but are externally applied and costly. DTs offer predictive capabilities but to date are typically not integrated with material behaviour. Looking at each in turn: The global composites market exceeds $120 billion and is projected to reach over $200 billion by 2033, driven by demand for lightweight, durable materials across construction, energy and mobility sectors 51. This market is dominated by large industrial players such as Toray, Teijin, Hexcel and Mitsubishi Chemical, whose competitive advantage lies in material performance and manufacturing scale52. However, these solutions are fundamentally linear, with limited capability for circularity, repairability or lifecycle intelligence. In parallel, the SHM market is emerging as a high -growth segment, expected to grow from $4.45 billion in 2025 to over $22 billion by 2034 53. This domain includes sensor providers and monitoring platforms focused on data acquisition and condition assessment. While these systems enable improved inspection and diagnostics, they are typically externally applied, data-centric, and not embedded into the material or manufacturing process, resulting in high deployment costs and limited integration with structural design. Regarding the DT-enabled infrastructure monitoring market, which, although smaller (~$280 million in 2025), is growing rapidly (14% CAGR) as asset owners shift toward predictive maintenance and lifecycle optimisation 54. Current solutions in thi s area focus on simulation, data fusion & predictive analytics, but are constrained by data availability, interoperability challenges, and weak coupling with material behaviour and manufacturing processes, limiting their ability to deliver prescriptive, real -time decision -making. More recently, the emergence of smart composites (i.e. materials integrating sensing, adaptive behaviour, and connectivity) points towards the convergence across these domains, with the market projected to exceed $700 billion by 20 3455. However, current developments are still largely at research or niche industrial levels, with limited deployment in civil infrastructure and no integrated lifecycle framework combining material design, manufacturing, monitoring, and circularity. A preliminary outline is provided here. Market size and potential : The European construction sector is undergoing a structural transition driven by the convergence of decarbonisation, digitalisation, and circular economy policies. Advanced composite materials, particularly FRPCs, are increasingly adopted due to their corrosion resistance, lightweight properties, and durability. The global FRPC market was valued at approximately €89 billion in 2023 and is projected to reach €129 billion by 2030, driven by demand for lightweight, corrosion -resistant structural materials across construction, transport, and energy sectors. Constructi on currently accounts for approximately 25% of FRPC consumption, representing an addressable market of roughly €22 billion that is expected to grow inline with the overall market trajectory. This construction FRPC segment sits within a broader €1.38 trilli on European construction market, in which advanced composites remain under-penetrated relative to steel and concrete. The convergence of regulatory pressure (revised CPR, ESPR, DBL and GPP criteria , etc. ) and performance advantages (corrosion resistance, design flexibility, reduced maintenance) positions intelligent circular composites as a high-growth segment within this market. MOSAIC targets three primary addressable market segments (each characterised by high exposure to environmental degradation, increasing adoption of composite alternatives, and strong demand for reduced maintenance) derived from these figures: Infrastructure rehabilitation and new build (bridges, facades, structural panels): European bridge infrastructure alone represents a maintenance and replacement market exceeding €10 billion annually (derived from the €130-150 billion annual infrastructure maintenance expenditure cited above, with bridge structures accounting for approximately 7-10%). MOSAIC's self-healing, reversible, and monitorable composite systems directly address this segment by offering ≥30% operational maintenance cost reduction and ≥20% service life extension. Prefabricated modular construction elements: The European prefabricated building market, a sub-segment of the €1.38 trillion construction sector, represents a growing opportunity for lightweight, circular composite modules that can be assembled, disassembled, and reconfigured. MOSAIC's reversible adhesive joints and modular design philosophy are directly aligned with this segment. Cross-sector replication (wind energy, automotive, marine): The vitrimer platform's circularity and self-healing properties address the €4-5billion European wind turbine blade market, where end-of-life composite waste is an acute regulatory and environmental challenge, as well as automotive and marine structural applications. This is a broad and scalable market opportunity. User needs & market gaps: Despite the growth of composites, critical gaps remain: 1) infrastructure owners face high maintenance costs and operational uncertainty due to limited real-time visibility of structural conditions. Maintenance remains reactive and inspection driven. 2) the lack of circularity in thermoset composites creates a major environmental and regulatory challenge, particularly as end-of-life waste increases. 3) existing solutions are fragmented. Materials, monitoring technologies and digital tools are developed independently, limiting integration and adoption. 4) regulatory & societal pressures are increasing, requiring transparency, safety, and sustainability across the full lifecycle of construction materials. MOSAIC addresses these gaps through the integration of self -healing materials, embedded sensing, AI-driven DT capabilities, and circularity frameworks into a unified system. VALUE PROPOSITION: MOSAIC delivers measurable value to asset owners: ≥30% lower maintenance costs, ≥20% longer service life, and recoverable material value at end -of-life—enabled by intelligent, self -healing and circular composite systems. Willingness to buy (WTB): The WTB for the MOSAIC solutions will be driven by a clear and quantifiable value proposition (VP) for infrastructure owners, contractors & asset managers, centred on lifecycle cost reduction, operational efficiency and regulatory compliance. In contrast to conventional materials, where procurement decisions are primarily based on upfront CAPEX, MOSAIC will enable a shift towards lifecycle -driven decision -making, where total cost of ownership, maintenance burden, and residual value become the dominant factors. This is particularly relevant in the context of increasing regulatory pressure and ageing infrastructure across Europe, where asset performance and durability directly impact long-term investment strategies. As such, the business case will be structured around the following economic value drivers: ▪ CAPEX/OPEX impact: Quantification of capital expenditure for vitrimer composite manufacturing lines (pultrusion, VARI, RTM) relative to conventional steel/concrete fabrication, and operational expenditure savings MOSAIC HORIZON-CL4-2025-05-MATERIALS-42-two-stage Page 29 of 53 derived from prescriptive maintenance (≥30% OPEX reduction target for operational maintenance costs enabled by prescriptive DT and self -healing) and from lifecycle-wide efficiency gains (>10% OPEX reduction target per LCSA scope, assessed following ISO 156 86-5). Initial estimates from the project's techno -economic models will be validated against demonstrator-level data. ▪ Lifecycle gains: Whole-life cost analysis based on LCCA (ISO 15686-5) incorporating extended service life (≥20% life extension), reduced maintenance interventions, and residual value recovery through reversible disassembly and chemical recycling (>96% fibre recovery, ≥80% property retention). The circularity premium -arising from reuse of components across ≥3 reprocessing cycles -will be quantified against conventional thermoset FRPCs and steel/concrete benchmarks. ▪ Payback period: Target payback of <5 years for building owners and infrastructure operators, accounting for material cost (<€10/kg for vitrimer resins), reduced labour requirements (>50% on -site labour reduction), accelerated construction (≥30% faster), and deferred replacement costs. The business case targets a payback period of <5 years for infrastructure operators, supported by the combined value of maintenance cost reduction (≥30% OPEX), extended service life (≥20%), and circularity premiums from component reuse and high-value fibre recovery. Competition landscape and competitive positioning: The current market is characterised by partial and fragmented solutions. Advanced composites offer performance but lack circularity. SHM systems provide data but are externally applied and costly. DTs offer predictive capabilities but to date are typically not integrated with material behaviour. This domain includes sensor providers and monitoring platforms focused on data acquisition and condition assessment. While these systems enable improved inspection and diagnostics, they are typically externally applied, data-centric, and not embedded into the material or manufacturing process, resulting in high deployment costs and limited integration with structural design. Regarding the DT-enabled infrastructure monitoring market, which, although smaller (~$280 million in 2025), is growing rapidly (14% CAGR) as asset owners shift toward predictive maintenance and lifecycle optimisation 54. Current solutions in thi s area focus on simulation, data fusion & predictive analytics, but are constrained by data availability, interoperability challenges, and weak coupling with material behaviour and manufacturing processes, limiting their ability to deliver prescriptive, real -time decision -making. 2.2 Measures to maximise impact - Dissemination, exploitation and communication We adopt a structured, audience-differentiated approach to dissemination, exploitation and communication (DEC), recognising that our project's results span a wide continuum, from fundamental alloy-design knowledge to validated demonstrator evidence packages and FAIR-compliant data assets. Our DEC plan is organised around three concentric rings: scientific dissemination (peer community), industrial exploitation (value-chain adopters, standards bodies, regulators), and public communication (policy audiences, general public). Activities in all three rings run in parallel from Month 3 onward, governed by our INFUSE DEC Plan (D8.1, due M6) and updated annually. 

2.2.1 Open Science and FAIR Data Management We operate under a default 'as open as possible, as closed as necessary' policy, consistent with Horizon Europe Annex 5 obligations. All peer-reviewed publications arising from our project will be deposited immediately in a CoreTrustSeal-certified open-access repository (Zenodo, EOSC node), with metadata released under CC0 and full texts under CC-BY. We target a minimum of 40 peer-reviewed publications over 48 months, with Open Research Europe used as the preferred publication platform for at least 30% of our outputs to ensure zero embargo and transparent peer review. Our INFUSE DMP (D8.2, M6, updated at M24) will govern the lifecycle of all generated datasets, including: (i) alloy composition –property screening datasets from Loops A, B and C; (ii) AI model weights, hyperparameters and uncertainty estimates; (iii) SSbD gate-decision records with their computational inputs; and (iv) demonstrator performance evidence packages (PEPs) with full traceability metadata. All datasets will be described using community-standard ontologies aligned with the European Materials Modelling Ontology (EMMO) and the OntoCommons framework, enabling machine-readable interoperability with the Materials Commons for Europe infrastructure. We explicitly plan to contribute curated, validated datasets to the Materials Commons for Europe platform (specifically, the LPBF process-window libraries (Loop A), the PVD target-qualification datasets (Loop B), and the thermal spray coating performance records (Loop C)) following the common materials taxonomy and provenance standards required for cross-supply-network interoperability. This contribution strengthens the European shared knowledge infrastructure and fulfils the call's expectation that digital tools and FAIR data support the circular transition of IAMs across supply networks. 

2.2.2 Scientific Dissemination Our scientific dissemination strategy targets three principal audiences: the materials modelling and HEA research community, the additive manufacturing and powder process engineering community, and the SSbD/sustainability-assessment community. The strategy rests on four complementary channels. Peer-reviewed publications. We commit to a minimum of 18 peer-reviewed articles across high-impact journals in the fields of materials science, additive manufacturing, and computational design (target journals include Acta Materialia, npj Computational Materials, Additive Manufacturing, Journal of Cleaner Production, and Green Chemistry). A minimum of 12 articles will report on Loops A –C results with accompanying open datasets. At least 3 articles will address transferability methodology and uncertainty quantification, making the scientific limits of HEA design-to-market acceleration a primary deliverable rather than an assumption. Conference presentations and workshops. We target presentation slots at the leading international forums in our field, including TMS Annual Meeting (Materials Design and Additive Manufacturing), EURO PM (European Powder Metallurgy Association), MS&T (Materials Science and Technology), and the European Materials Research Society (E-MRS) Spring/Fall Meetings. We will encourage individual researchers, especially young researchers, to join thematically fitting COST Actions, not only to broaden their network and attend workshops but also to reach out to the community, making our capabilities known to a broader relevant audience. We will also co-organise a dedicated symposium on Circular Alloy Design in Additive Manufacturing at a major European conference (target: E-MRS M30), bringing together academic and industrial partners to share our outcomes and gather community validation of the DBTL framework. We will actively seek participation in relevant COST Actions (e.g. CA19118 High-Performance Alloys, or successor networks) to broaden our dissemination reach and establish cross-project synergies within the European research area. Pre-print and open data releases. In line with recommended Horizon Europe open science practices, we will deposit preprints on arXiv (cond-mat/mtrl-sci) or ChemRxiv at submission time and will release accompanying datasets on Zenodo with DOI-linked provenance records. This ensures community access to results substantially before formal publication and avoids the information asymmetry driven by embargoes that can disadvantage SMEs and policy actors. Training and capacity building. We will deliver two international training schools (M18 and M42) on FAIR-compliant alloy data pipelines and AI-driven circularity assessment, with free attendance for early-career researchers and SME representatives. Tutorial datasets and worked Jupyter notebooks will be published with open access on our project's GitHub repository, providing a self-contained educational resource beyond our project's lifetime. 

2.2.3 Communication to Wider Audiences Our communication strategy is designed to build awareness and narrative around our core proposition, that circular advanced alloys can be brought to market faster, more safely, and with quantifiable environmental benefit, among non-specialist audiences, including policymakers, procurement officers, the general public, and secondary-school students. Project website and digital presence. A public-facing project website (project-infuse.eu, or an equivalent available domain to be confirmed at project start, Website hosting and domain maintenance will transition to institutional infrastructure at our coordinating organisation post-project, with long-term stewardship agreements and redundant copies of all platform documentation and datasets deposited in EOSC-compliant institutional repositories, registered M1) will serve as the primary hub for project news, publications, open datasets, and demonstrator case studies. A dedicated industrial portal within the site will provide simplified summaries of demonstrator performance evidence, designed to support procurement decisions without requiring specialist materials knowledge. We will maintain the website for at least 5 years post-project. Policy briefs and regulatory engagement. We will produce three policy briefs (M18, M36, M48) targeted at European Commission DG GROW, ECHA, and national competent authorities, summarising our contribution to SSbD operationalisation, CRM-reduction evidence, and standards-gap analysis. The SSbD gate-decision records and lifecycle assessment outcomes will be formatted for direct input into the Common Data Platform for Chemicals, consistent with the SRIP recommendation that safety and sustainability data be shared across the regulatory ecosystem. Specifically, we will submit to the Common Data Platform for Chemicals: (i) SSbD gate-decision records for all advancing alloy candidates, including hazard classification, exposure assessment inputs, and sustainability metrics, formatted in IUCLID-compatible structure (by M42); (ii) lifecycle assessment datasets per demonstrator loop, with impact category values and uncertainty ranges, in ILCD data format (by M48). NTUA (WP9 lead) will coordinate the data packaging and submission, in liaison with the JRC’s chemicals assessment unit. Outreach and science communication. We will engage science communication professionals (within at least two partner institutions) to produce accessible video and written content explaining circular alloy design for non-specialist audiences. Content will be distributed via LinkedIn (quarterly), YouTube (project documentary series, 4 episodes), and the Enterprise Europe Network to reach SME audiences across Europe. We will also contribute to the EU Industry Days and the European Research and Innovation Days through partner participation and poster contributions. Standardisation activities. We treat standardisation as a parallel-track exploitation activity rather than an afterthought, adopting the CWA pre-standardisation pathway proven in Horizon Europe materials projects. A Consortium Standardisation Group (CSG) comprising technical experts from IRIS, RM3, IlSent, MBN, AMAZEMET, and ŁPIT is established by M6 to scope the standardisation targets, recruit ≥10 external CWA participants from national standards bodies and AM/powder stakeholders, and maintain liaison with ISO/TC 261, CEN/TC 438, and ASTM F42 through our Industrial Advisory Board. The standards gap analysis (T10.3, M6 –M18) maps applicable ISO/ASTM standards for powder qualification, LPBF process qualification, coatings testing, and LCA, and identifies gaps that the CWA and pre-normative contributions must address. Two pre-normative contributions are prepared in the final project phase (T11.1, M37–M48): (1) a powder-reuse qualification protocol for HEA feedstock targeting ISO/TC 261 Working Group 1, and (2) secondary feedstock traceability requirements for AM targeting CEN/TC 438. In parallel, the CWA on qualification requirements for circular HEA powders is drafted from WP12 demonstrator evidence and submitted for CEN approval by M48 (D11.1). CSG members commit to continuing the standardisation process for up to three years post-project, supporting the CWA-to-EN transition and ISO/CEN ballot cycles.

Phase 1: Kick-off phase of DEC activity implementation to raise awareness about the project Phase 2: DEC activities to disseminate results, and to build links with other projects, stakeholders and the various actors across the ecosystem Phase 3: DEC activities to intensify results dissemination and planning the pathways to exploit and valorise our project outcomes and Key Exploitable Results (KERs). We will aim to ensure that the concept of the proposed project is well structured and demonstrated at higher scale (TRL6 -8) into relevant environment and is aligned with customer request or market needs. The exploitation pathways will be supported by prototype validation, LCA and techno- economic analyses, and engagement with standardisation and policy actors. The main exploitation pathways are as follows: ■ Commercial and industrial exploitation: Exploitation will be pursued at two complementary levels. At system level, the project will develop a use-case–driven techno-economic and deployment assessment for the integrated H2SAVE solution, including safety plan documentation and HAZID/HAZOP-informed operational guidelines supporting future certification and deployment, supporting future exploitation and scale-up decisions for critical infrastructure applications beyond the project duration. This assessment will identify key cost drivers, deployment constraints and potential exploitation pathways, without aiming at commercial readiness within the project. At component and subsystem level, individual Key Exploitable Results (KERs) (e.g. reversible PCEC stacks, control and digital twin solutions, system integration concepts) will be assessed for standalone exploitation potential or reuse in other application contexts, depending on partner strategies and market needs. This dual -level approach increases flexibility and resilience of exploitation, allowing consortium partners to pursue differentiated and complementary routes aligned with their technical profiles, market positioning and post-project ambitions. As part of the IP and exploitation planning, the partners will prepare for and arrive at an agreement to ensure the continuous/correct development of the concept in the post project to progress with higher TRL maturity (technology transfer, exploitation). ■ Training and skills development: Use of project knowledge and results to support training of engineers, operators and professionals in the safe and effective use of hydrogen-based backup systems. ■ Intellectual property protection: Protection of foreground IP through patents and other appropriate IPR instruments, in line with the project’s IP strategy , Consortium Agreement, and agreement between different partners to assess knowledge transfer and exploitation. ■ Scientific exploitation: Use of results as a foundation for further R&I activities, follow -up projects, scale -up initiatives and academic research. ■ Contribution to standards and policy: Standardisation engagement initiated during the project will continue beyond its end through sustained participation in relevant technical committees and follow -up contributions based on validated project results. Contribution to pre -standardisation activities, technical reports and policy - relevant discussions related to hydrogen systems, energy storage and critical infrastructure resilience, in line with EU Knowledge Valorisation principles. This includes insights on replacing diesel backup systems, integrating hydrogen storage, and enabling robust off-grid operation. Strategy for the management of intellectual property : H2SAVE will ensure protection and exploitation of commercially valuable innovations . Initial patentability studies using EPO & WIPO patent libraries have been conducted to confirm Freedom to Operate (FTO) and novelty related to reversible protonic ceramic electrochemical cells, system integration concepts, control strategies, and hydrogen storage architectures. These studies together with in-depth patent landscaping will continue throughout H2SAVE to ensure FTO for project outcomes considered for IP application and patenting. A continuous Technology Watch activity will be implemented to monitor advances in reversible PCEC technologies, hydrogen storage systems, thermal management solutions, digital twins, and control frameworks, informing the consortium of emerging IP, relevant publications, and competing developments. A complete IP strategy and associated IPR portfolio will be generated and maintained under Subtask 8.5.1/9.5.1/10.5.1 IPR Management and Technology Watch. The strategy is to file ≥3 new patents, alongside exploring other IPR tools (trademarks, copyright, Trade Secrets). Foreground IP is expected to arise from multiple partners, including patentable innovations related to reversible PCEC stack and hotbox design, hydrogen balance-of-plant integration, hybridisation with batteries, and digital decision-support tools for on-grid/off-grid operation. In addition, substantial shared know -how will be generated at system level, covering safe operation, thermal manag ement, resilience strategies and deployment in critical infrastructure contexts. IPR protection of commercially sensitive innovations will be balanced with open science to allow other EU researchers to build on H2SAVE results while preserving exploitation potential for industrial uptake, and adopting FAIR data principles ( Subtask 8.5.2/9.5.2/10.5.2 Data Management and FAIR compliance ). Knowledge Management will follow the Consortium Agreement (CA), specifying ownership, access rights, and conditions for Background and Foreground knowledge. Access will be granted royalty-free where needed for project activities and on fair conditions for further use, ensuring secure and fair exploitation across partners.
